region_of_origin: China (Yunnan Province); widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for the Singapore-style served rice noodle dish (米线); the closest defensible match is the generic rice noodle food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers rice noodles as a category; no specific code exists for the prepared dish with broth as served in Singapore.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for rice noodle dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve bowl of rice noodle soup as commonly consumed in Singapore hawker centres weighs approximately 350 g, comprising noodles, broth, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: 米线 Rice Noodle — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 米线 Rice Noodle (cr_id G00010516) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles, dried, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for the Singapore HPB 米线 dish as served. GI of 61 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (dried, boiled), which is the closest approved analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 25.19 g/100g = 88.2 g) / 100 = 53.8 g; however, a standard 350 g bowl contains approximately 120 g of noodle solids (not all broth), yielding available carbohydrate ≈ 22.1 g, GL ≈ 13.5. Estimation documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g (Na:K ratio < 1)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor total sodium intake from this dish, particularly if served with a high-sodium broth or sauce; request reduced-sodium broth and avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce to keep a single serving below 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~61), carbohydrate 25.2 g/100 g moderated by dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size to one standard bowl (approximately 350 g), pair with non-starchy vegetables and a lean protein topping to lower the overall glycaemic response, and avoid sweet or starchy broths that raise the effective GI of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (HPB does not report saturated fat separately; total fat 4.21 g/100 g is moderate), cholesterol confirmed 0 mg/100 g, dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rice noodle soup is a relatively heart-friendly staple due to negligible cholesterol and moderate total fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose low-sodium broth, avoid fried toppings, and ensure adequate vegetable and lean-protein accompaniments to support a cardioprotective dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate base with limited anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain rice noodles lack omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, and antioxidants required for RA suitability; pair with anti-inflammatory toppings such as salmon, tofu, or leafy greens and avoid high-sodium or chilli-based broths that may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 米线 (Mǐ Xiàn), known in English as Rice Noodle or Rice Noodle Soup, is a popular Chinese noodle dish originating from Yunnan Province, China, and widely consumed across Singapore's hawker centres and food courts. The dish consists of smooth, round rice-flour noodles served in a savoury broth, typically accompanied by protein toppings such as minced pork, sliced beef, fish balls, or tofu, and garnished with spring onions, bean sprouts, and chilli. The noodles have a soft, slightly chewy texture and a mild, neutral flavour that absorbs the surrounding broth. Per 100 g as served, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium, with notable potassium (327 mg), calcium (54 mg), magnesium (40 mg), and iron (2.55 mg). The glycaemic index is approximately 61 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The fibre content moderates the glycaemic response relative to plain white rice. Sodium is in the borderline range and is largely attributable to the broth; the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is confirmed at 0 mg/100 g. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets. It is a common lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Chinese hawker food culture.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of 米线?
Is 米线 suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of rice noodles?
How much sodium is in rice noodle soup?
Is rice noodle soup good for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for 米线 Rice Noodle (cr_id G00010516) with a reported confidence of 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for dried boiled rice noodles, clearly documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 米线 Rice Noodle (G00010516) as of 2026-04-16; detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient breakdown are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; rice noodles are a plant-derived product with no animal-fat component in the base noodle). 3. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (dried boiled rice noodles); no direct GI measurement exists for the Singapore HPB 米线 dish as served. Glycaemic load per serving calculated using estimated noodle-solid mass of approximately 120 g per 350 g bowl. 4. Sodium of 231 mg/100 g likely reflects the dish as served with broth; plain dried rice noodles have near-zero sodium. Users should verify whether the HPB figure represents noodle-only or full-dish composition. 5. Dietary flags: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false because the dish is typically served with meat or fish-based broth and protein toppings; the base noodle itself is vegan. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315595 is the closest defensible match for rice noodle food product; no exact FoodOn entry for the Singapore-style prepared dish exists. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest category-level match.
